notes.anchor: at Aquinum and at Fabrateria plots were laid against me
notes.note: Aquinum and Fabrateria (Fabrateria Vetus, in the Liri valley, modern Ceprano) are towns on the Via Latina south-east of Rome, near Cicero's estates at Arpinum; this passage is our only evidence of the plots, and the identity of the conspirators is unknown.

Vestricius Spurinna was the distinguished haruspex (diviner of entrails) who famously warned Julius Caesar to beware the Ides of March; Cicero here invokes him with comic irony as a prophet of Paetus's social doom.

Favonius (the west wind, Greek Zephyrus) was the mild spring breeze that traditionally arrived in late February and signalled the end of winter; Spurinna's mock-prophecy therefore threatens that Paetus must resume dinner-parties when spring comes.

Cicero's etymology contrasts Greek symposion (drinking-together) and syndeipnon (dining-together) with Latin convivium (living-together), arguing that the Roman term captures a richer idea of communal life than the Greek words, which stress merely eating and drinking.

The Latin word convivium derives from con- (together) + vivere (to live); Cicero treats this folk-etymology as philosophically significant, connecting the dinner-party to the Stoic and Epicurean ideals of the shared happy life.

notes.anchor: day and night I do nothing else, care for nothing else, except that my fellow citizens should be safe and free
notes.note: Writing in early 43 BC, Cicero is at the height of his campaign against Mark Antony; the Philippics (speeches to the Senate) and his intense correspondence with provincial governors are the practical expression of this stated purpose.
